issue: `.env.agent-swarm` containing real API keys (DeepSeek `sk-97d8...`, Telegram bot token `8697266775:AAE9...`) is tracked in git and committed to history.
suggestion: Rotate both keys immediately, remove file from git with `git filter-repo`, and add `.env.agent-swarm` to `.gitignore`.
why: Anyone with repo access can extract live API keys from git history, enabling unauthorized API usage and Telegram bot hijacking.

issue: Webhook verification tokens for WhatsApp (line 281) and Meta (line 482) use `==` comparison instead of `secrets.compare_digest()`, enabling timing attacks to extract tokens character-by-character.
suggestion: Replace `hub_verify_token == expected_token` with `secrets.compare_digest(hub_verify_token or '', expected_token)` on both lines 281 and 482.
why: An attacker measuring response times can recover the webhook verification token and register a malicious webhook endpoint.

issue: Rate limiter only covers `/api/*` paths; web login (`/auth/login`), vendor login (`/vendor/auth/login`), registration (`/auth/register`), password reset (`/auth/forgot-password`), and MFA verification endpoints have zero rate limiting.
suggestion: Add a `WebAuthRateLimitMiddleware` that applies stricter limits (e.g., 10 req/min per IP) to `/auth/login`, `/auth/register`, `/auth/forgot-password`, `/auth/mfa`, `/vendor/auth/login`, and `/vendor/auth/forgot-password`.
why: Without rate limiting on auth endpoints, attackers can brute-force passwords, enumerate accounts via registration, or spam password reset emails.

issue: Vendor portal sessions are stored in an in-memory dict (`_VENDOR_SESSIONS = {}`) that is lost on process restart and not shared across Gunicorn/Uvicorn workers.
suggestion: Replace `_VENDOR_SESSIONS` dict with Redis-backed or database-backed session storage, consistent with how admin sessions use the `AuthSession` model.
why: In multi-worker deployments, vendor users randomly lose their sessions when requests hit a different worker, and all sessions vanish on restart.

issue: Vendor auth cookies are hardcoded `secure=True` (lines 69, 77, 90, 178, 234) while admin/customer portals use configurable `settings.cookie_secure`, breaking vendor login in HTTP development environments.
suggestion: Replace all hardcoded `secure=True` in `web_vendor_auth.py` with `secure=settings.cookie_secure` to match the admin portal pattern.
why: Inconsistent cookie security: vendor portal is broken in dev (HTTP), and the hardcoded value cannot be configured per environment.

issue: MFA tokens (JWT with 5-min TTL) are not invalidated after successful MFA verification — the same token can be reused to authenticate multiple times within its validity window.
suggestion: Track consumed MFA tokens in Redis or a DB table; reject tokens that have already been verified successfully.
why: An intercepted MFA token allows repeated authentication bypass within its 5-minute window.

issue: TOTP verification (`valid_window=0`) does not track previously used codes — the same valid TOTP code can be replayed multiple times within the 30-second window.
suggestion: Store the last accepted TOTP code or timestamp per MFA method in Redis/DB; reject codes that match the previously consumed value.
why: An attacker who intercepts a valid TOTP code can replay it within the same 30-second window for additional authentications.

issue: Default database URL contains hardcoded credentials `postgres:postgres@localhost:5434/dotmac_crm` — if `DATABASE_URL` env var is unset, the app silently connects with default dev credentials.
suggestion: Remove the default value and raise a clear error if `DATABASE_URL` is not set: `database_url: str = os.environ['DATABASE_URL']`.
why: Accidental deployment without `DATABASE_URL` silently uses dev credentials, potentially connecting to wrong databases or exposing weak auth.

issue: Legal document `list()` uses `getattr(LegalDocument, order_by, ...)` for dynamic column ordering without an allowlist, unlike the 30+ other service files that use `apply_ordering()`.
suggestion: Replace the `getattr()` pattern with `apply_ordering(query, order_by, order_dir, allowed_columns)` from `app/services/common.py`.
why: While currently unexposed to user input, this is the only service using an unsafe ordering pattern — if a route ever passes user input it becomes an ORM injection vector.

issue: API login endpoint (`POST /api/v1/auth/login`) relies on global rate limiter (100 req/min per IP) which is far too generous for an authentication endpoint — allows ~500 password attempts in the 5-min lockout window.
suggestion: Add a dedicated auth rate limiter (e.g., 5 req/min per IP) on the `/auth/login` and `/auth/mfa/verify` API endpoints.
why: Per-account lockout at 5 attempts doesn't prevent credential stuffing across multiple accounts; per-IP rate limiting on auth endpoints is the standard defense.

issue: Temporary passwords generated for new users (`secrets.token_urlsafe(16)`, lines 1753 and 1787) are returned in the page context and displayed to the admin — there is no forced password change on first login for these auto-generated credentials.
suggestion: Set `must_change_password=True` on the newly created credential so the user is forced to change the temporary password on first login.
why: Admins may share temp passwords insecurely; forcing a change ensures only the end user knows the final password.

issue: Media URL signing falls back through `MEDIA_URL_SECRET -> JWT_SECRET -> SECRET_KEY` chain — reusing JWT_SECRET for media signatures means a JWT key compromise also compromises media URLs.
suggestion: Require an explicit `MEDIA_URL_SECRET` env var with no fallback to JWT_SECRET; raise a clear error if unset.
why: Sharing secret material across subsystems increases blast radius of any single key compromise.

issue: Registration endpoint has no CAPTCHA or bot-prevention mechanism — any automated script can create unlimited accounts.
suggestion: Add a CAPTCHA (hCaptcha/Turnstile) or honeypot field to the registration form to prevent automated account creation.
why: Automated account creation enables spam, resource exhaustion, and abuse of authenticated features.

issue: Password validation only enforces minimum length (8 chars) — no complexity requirements (uppercase, digit, special character) despite the model supporting `must_change_password`.
suggestion: Add a Pydantic validator on `new_password` fields requiring at least one uppercase, one digit, and one special character, or use a password strength library like `zxcvbn`.
why: Minimum-length-only passwords are vulnerable to dictionary attacks; complexity requirements or strength scoring significantly increase brute-force resistance.
